sp.p: (with deadly determination) I would kill him.

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: (insidiously) Come another glass, (Fogg drinks) I know that person ?

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: (quickly) Ah!

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: It is a woman ?

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: A woman ? What cause !

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: Jealousy !—jealousy of your Maria, because less beauteous, she envied—hated her !

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: (eagerly) Ah, well—this woman—this woman?

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: This very night, you may, perhaps, behold her.

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: (wildly) To-night?—where—where?

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: Not very distant. There is a lonely lane near the Clapham-road, close to the railway.

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: Ah, the King's-cross—the—brickfields—I know them—the "Ivy," I know it well.

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: Should she pass that way, she will clap her hands in private signal, and will confess if asked, she saw Maria Johnson die—by that you'll know her.

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: Enough, nothing shall save her ? (going.)

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: Stay, where are you going, (crosses.)

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: (doggedly) To wait for her.

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: Stop! take that liquor with you? (points to decanter.)

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: No ! I will take this ? (takes knife from table) Good bye !—good bye ! (rushes off.)

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: So that's accomplished ? That footstep ! Ah ! she comes!

div.stage: Enter Louisa, R.

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: (aside) I have given them the slip, their revelry, their gaiety, ill assimilated with my lonely thoughts. Now for this stranger ? What can he want with me ? He spoke of Herbert!

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: (advancing) Your servant, madam. I requested this interview by the desire of a common friend, Mr. Francis Danvers !

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: Ah, the generous, the devoted Frank! I hope no fatal accident––

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: No, madam; you have nothing to fear, at least on his account.

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: For whom else, then ?

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: The unhappy Herbert!

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: Great heavens!

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: He is obliged to conceal himself, to leave London. But I'll be brief. Heavy embarrassments, which his poor brother and I have in vain endeavoured to meet, have brought on him the harpies of the law. See madam, (showing bill) here is an acceptance drawn by Mr. Shabner for ten thousand pounds, and there are others which he's now pursued for.

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: Great heavens ! Oh, Herbert, Herbert! Yes ; 'tis too evident.

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: Mr. Frank wishes you immediately to leave this town for Liverpool—go by the rail—wait there for Herbert —he'll join you there.

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: Go without seeing him? Never; if he's in danger 'tis my duty to remain near him ; I will not leave London unless in company with Herbert.

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: But should it be impossible, should his safety render it necessary that he should quit London alone––

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: You'll excuse my apparent want of confidence— but I repeat, that I will not leave London without the fullest conviction that he will not marry another.

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: Well, then, you shall see him, there is where he is waiting.

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: (reading) "The railway, Clapham-road," ah, let me fly—you are certain I shall find him there ?

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p:
Yes ; or a man who will conduct you to him—
you'll find out this man, for it is necessary to use caution, by clapping your hands thrice near the house, and repeating the mysterious sesame, we have agreed on— these words "I saw Maria Johnson die!"

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: (starting, much agitated) Good heavens ! those words, those ominous words—Maria Johnson!

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: Do they frighten you ?

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: No, no—if Herbert has fixed on them.

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: He has!

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: Then I'll repeat them.

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: Do, they'll bring you to his arms, (loud laugh without.)

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: Those sounds—they come. Adieu! I'll fly to meet him. (exit Louisa)

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: What now shall cross me ? The brother lies in death among the ruins—that man will make sure of the woman—let me complete my work—for fortune, Dorrington and Herbert. (exit at back)

div.head: SCENE SECOND.—
div.stage: A gloomy cellar in the house of Shabner.
Enter Frank, R.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: A prisoner here in this lonesome place, whilst the villain, Devereux, revels in his guilt; no outlet—no means of escape. Oh, brother, brother, to what has thy folly and imprudence brought thee! Could I but defeat the ruffians, and prevent the consummation of their base designs. When I think that Herbert has consented to personate me, and in my name seeks to bring destruction upon an innocent girl, my heart swells nigh to bursting. (noise heard—music) What noise is that—it sounds if some one were trying to remove the bricks of this wall—should it be deliverance! should it be a friend! Oh, heaven grant it—that I may escape to save a brother from shame and ruin, and hurl a deadly retribution on the head of Devereux. (chord as a portion of the wall falls in, and Bob Yorkney, smothered in mud and dirt, falls through.)

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: It's only me, governor ; are you dead ? because, if you are, say so, and save me from wriggling through any further.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: That voice—I cannot be mistaken—Yorkney.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p:
Yes, Yorkney, who in spite of all those infernal blackguards did to prevent him, fought through every
obstacle, and is determined to snatch you from your enemies.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Thanks, thanks.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: And I had a job to find you arter they put you down the trap. Lor, I had two or three mills, was thrown out of the house like a dog, but I crawled through the sewers, for I knew where they'd put you. I knocked in the wall, and here I am with a muscle as big as the dome of St. Paul's, to floor any vagabond that dares molest you.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: But the villain Devereux ?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I overheard him hire old Fogg to kill your brother's young woman—to-morrow Herbert's to be married to Miss Dorrington, in your name, and your goose is supposed to be cooked.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Can you lead me to him ?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I can, but it's a lonely place.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: No, matter, villany must not triumph—I will snatch my brother from infamy, and save a confiding girl from despair. Come, Bob, and heaven will nerve the arm of Frank Danvers to fight in the cause of Innocence, and punish the guilty as their crimes deserved, (music— they exeunt through wall.)

div.head: SCENE THIRD.—
div.stage: The Railway, full set. Stage dark. Music.
Enter Fogg, he looks cautiously round.

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: This is the spot. Yes, there is the crib, the "Ivy,"—no one is here—good, good—but will she really come, this murderess ? Should I have been deceived—but no, no, 'tis clear, that man knows all—I'll have revenge!—he says I ought to have it. (grasping his knife firmly—walks about, agitated.)

div.stage: Music. Enter Louisa, cautiously looking round.

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: What a gloomy spot. I shudder, my courage fails me.

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: A woman! it must be her, let me see. (rapidly lays hold of her.)

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: (shrieks) Ah ! who are you ? what would you ?

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: (surprised) Who do I see ? I know you, yes, yes, the bridge, 'twas you who would have destroyed yourself.

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: And you are he who helped to save me.

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: (wildly) But what do you here?

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: I am in search of one—this should be the house.

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: No, no, not that, away, away and leave me ; I, too, am waiting, expecting some one ; you must not stay I could not before you–

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: (aside) He waiting, can he be the person who is to conduct me to Herbert ? I must ascertain that—(goes to house.)

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: (aside, agitated) Going there ? Great Heavens!

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: (aside) Now for the signal, (claps hands )

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: That signal—no, no, and yet what do you do there ? Why did you clap your hands ? Oh, if it should be ? (seizes and brings her down.)

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: Ah! did I not say that I would see some one ?

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: (with strong emotion) Who, who ? there is nobody here but me—we are alone, I tell you.

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: (aside) 'Tis plain he knows the secret, but waits the word—this is the man that's to conduct me, I'll speak to him at once, (aloud) Listen, my friend : "I saw Maria, Johnson Die!" (solemnly.)

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: (with a wild shriek, raises knife) Ah, wretch !

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: (screams) Oh, mercy !

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: (furiously) Repeat it, repeat it! Had any other said those words but you—I know not why I tremble—you did not—you did not—no, no, say no.

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: You doubt my truth? Again I say "I saw Maria Johnson die! "

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: (with desperate energy) Then I no longer pity! Perish!

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: (falling on knees) Oh, mercy! Yes, I saw her die, she—she was my mother!

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: (drops knife) Your mother! your mother! Merciful Providence!

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: That earnest gaze—what horrid mystery!

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: Your mother ! I cannot speak, I'm stifling—joy —gratitude—girl, girl—that woman, Maria Johnson your mother ! She was, she was !

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: Oh, speak!

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: My wife !

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: (throwing herself in his arms) Father, father ! (chord.)

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: Father ! that name heard once again! oh, it repays me all. Daughter, daughter ! (embraces her passionately.)

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: We never will part more.

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: Never, never!

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p:
My mother blessed you with her dying breath.
She knew you were not guilty. She had obtained the proof. It cost her her life ; the wretch for whose guilt you were condemned, to buy her silence and his safety murdered her.

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: Great heavens, my poor Maria!

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: She had discovered the culprit, whose real name was Bernard Thornton.

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: Bernard Thornton ? I do not know him—oh, if I did!

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: She never ceased till she had traced him out— he knew this, and he murdered her.

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: Oh, should I ever find that wretch ! aye, and that monster, Devereux—he who would have made me kill my child!

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: 'Twas he who sent me here, and bade me speak those words, little suspecting who I really was.

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: I see it all—oh, villain! villain!

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: Hush! I hear footsteps! Some one comes.

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: Ah, 'tis himself—'tis Devereux. He would be certain that his work of blood was done. Leave me, my child. (picks up knife.)

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: (alarmed) What would you do, my father ?

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: Leave me, I say, leave me. (Music—forces Louisa off, then hastens to meet Devereux, who enters.)

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: Is all finished ?

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: Not all; one act of justice yet remains—your death. (seizes him.)

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: Ah, madman!

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: (re-entering hastily) Great heaven! father, what would you do ?

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: Away, my child. Die, villain!

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: So, Master Fogg, this is how you serve me, is it? but I'll soon make short work of you.

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: Come on, villain, a father's arm is strong when he fights to protect his child.

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: Indeed. Take that! (suddenly stabs Fogg, who falls.)

sp.speaker: Louisa.
sp.p: Monster, you have killed him. Father, father! (crawling to him with a faint cry, sinks senseless.)

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p:
Now for my masterstroke, this girl once removed. Herbert and the fortune will be scarcely mine. A thought—I have some chloroform in my pocket—it will prevent her further interferences, (he draws from his pocket and places it to Louisa's nostrils) Ha, ha! now for master stroke — the down express is due in a
few minutes, and railway accidents are not at all impossible. (raises body of Louisa, places her neck across the rail) So much for her—now to wait at a distance the consummation of my plan. (music—he exits)

sp.speaker: Fogg.
sp.p: (reviving) Where am I? I thought I had been stabbed, what fearful place is this, and why have they brought me here. There is a mist before my eyes I cannot distinguish an object, (the red signal light at back is changed to green) Where is my child gone ? Oh, my old limbs are strengthless, nerveless, I cannot move, (train is seen to pass at back —green lime light on Louisa) Oh, heaven, must I die, and not again behold my child! (noise of train—red light on signal post by side of tunnel changes to white) Where is she ? I can hear the rattling of the train—but still cannot see my child, (red glare seen in the tunnel—he suddenly sees Louisa) Ah, she is there, upon the rails—she will be dashed to pieces ! Oh, let me save her, let me save her ! (train approaches; he just succeeds in rolling Louisa from it as train dashes from tunnel and off.)

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: Ha, ha! all is over. (Frank enters.)

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: No, villain! you are deceived—from too great a distance to prevent it—I witnessed your crime and you shall now answer to the laws for your attempt at murder ! Help! help! (seizes him.)

sp.speaker: Dever.
sp.p: Unhand me, or by hell I'll shoot you like a dog ! (draws a pistol and points it at Frank, who seizes the barrel —a violent struggle ensues.)

div.stage: Enter Yorkney, with Shabner, Railway Officials, &c., with lanterns—the pistol goes off, killing Devereux who falls dead—grand tableau—Fogg embracing Louisa— Herbert kneeling at her feet, Frank standing with the pistol over Devereux.
CURTAIN.
